content.problemStatement:
Consider an array AAA of size NNN: A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč, where NNN is even. The AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo of this array AAA, is another array BBB, which is computed as follows:


Start with an empty array BBB.
For iii ranging from 111 to N2\frac{N}{2}2N‚Äč, insert the elements (Ai+Ai+N2)(A_i + A_{i+\frac{N}{2}})(Ai‚Äč+Ai+2N‚Äč‚Äč) and ‚ą£Ai‚ąíAi+N2‚ą£|A_i - A_{i+\frac{N}{2}}|‚ą£Ai‚Äč‚ąíAi+2N‚Äč‚Äč‚ą£ to the end of array BBB. Here ‚ą£x‚ą£|x|‚ą£x‚ą£ refers to absolute value of xxx.
Rearrange the array BBB in some random order.
As you can see, because of the third point, an array AAA can have multiple AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo arrays.

Given an array BBB of even length NNN, your job is to find if there exists an array AAA such that BBB is the AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo of AAA. And if it does exist, find such an array AAA with the maximum possible sum of elements.

That is, if there is no array AAA such that BBB is an AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo of AAA, print ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1. Else print an array AAA such that BBB is an AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo of AAA, and the sum of the elements of this array AAA is as large as possible. If there are multiple arrays possible with the maximum sum, print any.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚ÄĒ the number of elements in the array.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,‚Ä¶,BNB_1,B_2,\ldots ,B_NB1‚Äč,B2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,BN‚Äč ‚ÄĒ the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, either ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1, or NNN space separated integers denoting array AAA. If there are multiple arrays possible with the maximum sum, print any.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3‚čÖ1053\cdot 10^53‚čÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111: Consider the array A=[7,5,3,3,3,1]A = [7, 5, 3, 3, 3, 1]A=[7,5,3,3,3,1].
Let us try to compute the AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo of this array by following the steps:

For i=1,7+3=10i=1, 7+3=10i=1,7+3=10 and ‚ą£7‚ąí3‚ą£=4|7-3|=4‚ą£7‚ąí3‚ą£=4
For i=2,5+3=8i=2, 5+3=8i=2,5+3=8 and ‚ą£5‚ąí3‚ą£=2|5-3|=2‚ą£5‚ąí3‚ą£=2
For i=3,3+1=4i=3, 3+1=4i=3,3+1=4 and ‚ą£3‚ąí1‚ą£=2|3-1|=2‚ą£3‚ąí1‚ą£=2

So, an AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo of AAA would be a rearrangement of the array [10,4,8,2,4,2][10, 4, 8, 2, 4, 2][10,4,8,2,4,2]. We see that the input array is indeed a rearrangement of this, and so, AAA is a valid array. It also turns out that this sum of 7+5+3+3+3+1=227 + 5 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 1 = 227+5+3+3+3+1=22, is the largest possible sum.
Test Case 222: Consider the array A=[4,4,0,1]A = [4, 4, 0, 1]A=[4,4,0,1].
Let us try to compute the AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo of this array by following the steps:

For i=1,4+0=4i=1, 4+0=4i=1,4+0=4 and ‚ą£4‚ąí0‚ą£=4|4-0|=4‚ą£4‚ąí0‚ą£=4
For i=2,4+1=5i=2, 4+1=5i=2,4+1=5 and ‚ą£4‚ąí1‚ą£=3|4-1|=3‚ą£4‚ąí1‚ą£=3

So, an AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo of AAA would be a rearrangement of the array [4,4,5,3][4, 4, 5, 3][4,4,5,3]. We see that the input array is indeed a rearrangement of this, and so, AAA is a valid array. It also turns out that this sum of 4+4+0+1=94 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 94+4+0+1=9, is the largest possible sum.
Test Case 333: It can be proved that there is no such array AAA whose AlterEgoAlterEgoAlterEgo is the given array BBB.
